perturbation.original_text: The Receiving party shall not use the Confidential information to procure a commercial advantage and/or otherwise for any purpose whatsoever other than the Purpose without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving party shall try to avoid using the Confidential information to directly procure a clear commercial advantage for purposes clearly outside the Purpose without explicit disapproval communicated by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text forbids using confidential information for commercial advantage. The modified version introduces multiple ambiguities: "try to avoid", "directly procure a clear commercial advantage", "clearly outside the Purpose", and "explicit disapproval". This makes the obligation unclear and difficult to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 405 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (Criminal Breach of Trust)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 405 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 405 of the Indian Penal Code defines Criminal Breach of Trust, which includes misusing entrusted property (like confidential information) in violation of a contract. The original text strictly prohibits using the confidential information to procure commercial advantage, aligning with preventing a criminal breach of trust. The modified text, with its weakened and ambiguous language, makes it harder to determine if a criminal breach of trust has occurred, thus contradicting the intent and enforceability of Section 405.

CLOSE Submit Home INDIAN PENAL CODE SECTION 405 हिंदी में पढ़िए IPC Section 405 - Criminal breach of trust हिंदी में पढ़िए Last Updated: 01 Jul, 2025 By Advocate Chikirsha Mohanty As per new Indian Criminal law, IPC Section 405 has been replaced with BNS Section 316 with effect from July 1, 2024. (Definition presented in BNS as a sub-section.) Please refer to BNS 316 for updated procedures & punishments. Table of Contents IPC 405 in Simple Words Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits “criminal breach of trust”. IPC 405 in Simple Words When someone is entrusted with property but dishonestly misuses or converts it for their own benefit, it is considered "criminal breach of trust" according to section 405 of the Indian Penal Code. 641+ Lawyers are online Connect with a criminal lawyer in one minute Criminal breach of trust Get the full list of all sections in the Indian Penal Code (IPC) here. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the original obligation, making it difficult to enforce the prohibition against using confidential information for commercial advantage. The original text strictly prohibits misuse, while the changed text allows for interpretation, potentially undermining the purpose of preventing criminal breach of trust as outlined in the Law Explanation referencing Section 405 of the Indian Penal Code. The provided snippet from Lawrato.com regarding IPC Section 405 describes 'dishonestly misuses or converts it for their own benefit,' which the original text aimed to prevent. The modified text makes it harder to prove dishonest misuse, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereto undertake that any dispute which may arise between them shall first be dealt with in the manner stated below, irrespective of the other recourse, which any Party may have in law or in equity. 14.1 If any dispute arises between the Parties hereto during the subsistence of this Agreement or thereafter, in connection with the validity, interpretation, implementation or alleged material breach of any provision of this Agreement or regarding a question, including the questions as to whether the termination of this Agreement by one Party hereto has been legitimate, both Parties hereto shall endeavour to settle such dispute amicably. If the Parties fail to bring about an amicable settlement within a period of thirty (30) days, either Party to the dispute may give ten (10) days notice of invocation of dispute settlement by the Film Writers Association, Mumbai, to the other Party in writing.The Parties hereto shall submit to such mediation award by the Film Writers Association and the award shall be enforceable in any competent court of law in Mumbai.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereto will consider attempting to resolve any dispute that might arise informally. Should disputes arise during the term of this Agreement, related to the validity, interpretation, implementation, or alleged breach, the Parties may explore amicable settlements. If amicable settlement seems unfeasible after some time, either Party might optionally suggest mediation by the Film Writers Association, Mumbai. The Parties will consider the recommendations of the Film Writers Association.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates dispute resolution through the Film Writers Association. The modified text uses vague language like "consider attempting," "may explore," "seems unfeasible," "might optionally suggest," and "will consider the recommendations", transforming a mandatory obligation into a series of discretionary actions. This introduces significant ambiguity about the actual dispute resolution process.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 89 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, India
perturbation.law_citation: Section 89 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 89 of the Code of Civil Procedure encourages Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR), but it does not negate the enforceability of a contractually agreed-upon dispute resolution mechanism. By weakening the mandatory nature of the FWA's mediation and award, the modified text contradicts the spirit of ensuring efficient dispute resolution, potentially leading to delays and increased litigation costs. While ADR is encouraged, a clear contractual obligation to a specific ADR method should be upheld, which the modification undermines.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: India Code: Invalid URL or Argument(s). Invalid URL or Argument(s). The system has experienced an error. Please try to do what you were doing again, and if the problem persists, please contact us so we can fix the problem. Go to the DSpace home page
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates dispute resolution through the Film Writers Association, while the modified text makes this process discretionary. The Perturbation Explanation clearly states that mandatory obligations were weakened with phrases like 'consider attempting' and 'may explore.' The Law Explanation indicates that weakening a contractually agreed-upon dispute resolution mechanism contradicts the spirit of efficient dispute resolution. While Snippet 1 is empty and Snippet 2 provides an error message, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation strongly suggest the change from mandatory to discretionary dispute resolution introduces ambiguity, potentially leading to non-compliance. The definition of 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' directly applies here, as the change introduces ambiguity, making it difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: All obligations respecting the Confidential information already provided hereunder shall survive in perpetuity after the date that the specific Confidential information was first disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: Obligations regarding the Confidential information provided under this agreement should ideally continue for a very long time after disclosure, if reasonably practical.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing “shall survive in perpetuity” with “should ideally continue for a very long time...if reasonably practical” introduces ambiguity regarding the duration of confidentiality obligations. "Very long time" is vague, and "if reasonably practical" makes the obligation conditional and subjective.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_citation: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act renders agreements in restraint of trade void, with some exceptions. While confidentiality agreements are generally enforceable, an excessively broad or indefinite duration can be construed as an unreasonable restraint of trade. The original 'in perpetuity' could be challenged but is a clearer intent. Changing it to 'should ideally continue for a very long time, if reasonably practical' opens the door to interpretations that could render the clause unenforceable due to vagueness and subjectivity regarding the duration and circumstances under which the confidentiality obligation exists, potentially creating uncertainty about the enforceability of the restraint.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that confidentiality obligations survive 'in perpetuity', indicating a clear and definite duration. The changed text replaces this with 'should ideally continue for a very long time...if reasonably practical,' which introduces significant ambiguity and conditionality. The 'very long time' is vague, and the 'if reasonably practical' clause makes the obligation subjective and dependent on circumstances. According to the Law Explanation, this vagueness and subjectivity could render the clause unenforceable, potentially creating uncertainty about the enforceability of the restraint. Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, as mentioned in the Law Explanation, deals with agreements in restraint of trade, and the altered text's ambiguity could be seen as an unreasonable restraint. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Mines Act, which, although not directly applicable to confidentiality agreements, underscores the importance of clarity and precision in legal obligations. The change from a definite 'in perpetuity' to a vague and conditional statement significantly weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality obligation, creating a legal contradiction.
